ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pancake food product class; no specific entry for pancakes with maple syrup topping. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GG covers pancakes as a grain-based snack product; maple syrup topping not separately coded. Closest parent match used for the pancake base.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pancakes with maple syrup; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two medium pancakes (approximately 150 g total) drizzled with maple syrup, commonly consumed as a breakfast or snack item.

nutrient_source.title: Pancakes with Maple Syrup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11100020 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB source record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 67 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain pancakes made from packet mix (GI ~67, medium category). Maple syrup has a GI of approximately 54 (low-medium); the combined dish GI is estimated at 67 reflecting the dominant refined-flour pancake base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 34.74 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 34.9 g carbohydrate per 150 g serving × 67 / 100 ≈ 15.5 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.9 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.69 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium is 83 mg/100 g and potassium is only 24 mg/100 g, yielding an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and reduce or eliminate maple syrup, as the high sugar load promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and the low potassium content (24 mg/100 g) does not offset cardiovascular risk; pairing with fresh fruit can improve the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (34.74 g/100 g) with sugar at 10.9 g/100 g and low dietary fibre (1.69 g/100 g) produces a medium-to-high glycaemic response; the refined wheat flour base combined with maple syrup sucrose elevates post-meal blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food due to its high refined carbohydrate and sugar content (34.74 g carbohydrate, 10.9 g sugar per 100 g) and low fibre (1.69 g/100 g); if consumed, portion should be small, maple syrup replaced with cinnamon or fresh berries, and wholemeal flour substituted to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar from maple syrup (10.9 g/100 g total sugar) exceeds AHA recommended daily added-sugar thresholds per serving; refined flour base contributes to elevated triglycerides and LDL when consumed regularly; cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g per HPB source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, regular consumption of pancakes with maple syrup is inadvisable due to the high added-sugar content (10.9 g/100 g) which is associated with elevated triglycerides and LDL cholesterol; portion control, substitution of wholemeal flour, and elimination of maple syrup are recommended to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (10.9 g/100 g) with low fibre (1.69 g/100 g) and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, negligible polyphenols, fibre below 3 g/100 g) fails the RA positive-criterion requirement.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food as its high sugar and refined carbohydrate content promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; replacing maple syrup with fresh berries or walnuts and using wholemeal flour would improve the anti-inflammatory profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pancakes with maple syrup is a Western-origin snack and breakfast dish consisting of soft, pan-fried or griddle-cooked flat cakes made from a batter of wheat flour, eggs, milk, butter, baking powder, and salt, served with maple syrup drizzled on top. The pancakes have a light, fluffy interior and a lightly golden exterior, with a mildly sweet, buttery flavour complemented by the rich, caramel-like sweetness of maple syrup. Per 100 g, the dish provides 232 kcal, 7.03 g protein, 6.94 g fat, 34.74 g carbohydrate, 10.9 g sugar, 1.69 g dietary fibre, and 83 mg sodium. The high refined carbohydrate and sugar content, combined with low dietary fibre, results in a medium glycaemic index of approximately 67 and a glycaemic load of approximately 15.5 per 150 g serving. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar load, low fibre, and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients. Calcium content is notable at 99 mg/100 g, contributed by the milk and flour base. The dish is commonly consumed as a breakfast, brunch, or snack item across North America, Australia, and Singapore, and is widely available in cafes and fast-food outlets. Allergens include gluten, wheat, eggs, and dairy. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan or gluten-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11100020, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for plain pancakes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pancakes with maple syrup is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F11100020. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB source record for this food item. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as explicitly reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this record (confirmed zero per HPB). GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain pancakes from packet mix; maple syrup GI (~54) is lower but the refined flour base dominates the combined GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (67 × 52.11 g carbohydrate per 150 g serving) / 100 ≈ 15.5. This food is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null. The food is classified as Western/American origin and the G6 multilingual rule for Singaporean dishes does not apply.
